Algebraic topology

Turning continuous deformations into algebra — loops, coverings, chain complexes, and the Euler characteristic. A tour of how the global shape of a space is recorded in finite algebraic data.

blocks.html:
1. From shapes to invariants

Algebraic topology is the business of replacing a space $X$ — a gelatinous continuous thing — by algebraic invariants: groups, rings, vector spaces. The translation is functorial: a continuous map $f\colon X\to Y$ gets sent to a homomorphism $f_*\colon \pi_1(X)\to\pi_1(Y)$ or $f_*\colon H_n(X)\to H_n(Y)$. The decisive property is that these invariants are homotopy invariants: continuously deforming the map does not change the induced algebraic map.

Paths and homotopy

A path in $X$ is a continuous map $\gamma:[0,1]\to X$, and a homotopy between paths is a continuous deformation through paths. Passing to homotopy classes turns geometric motion into algebraic data, first for loops (yielding $\pi_1$), then for chains (yielding $H_n$).

See also
  • Continuity & homeomorphism
Used in
Algebraic topology · Fundamental group
Algebraic topology · Simply connected domains
Analytic continuation · Continuation along a path
Complex Analysis · Contour integrals

Simply connected spaces

A space is simply connected if it is path-connected and every loop contracts to a point, equivalently $\pi_1(X)=0$. This hypothesis appears repeatedly in analysis and geometry (for example, contour integration on simply connected domains).

See also
  • Riemann mapping theorem
Used in
Analytic continuation · Monodromy theorem
Complex Analysis · Cauchy's theorem

Why would you want to do this? Because continuous problems become combinatorial the moment you apply a functor. Three classic examples:

Brouwer fixed point. Every continuous $f\colon D^n\to D^n$ has a fixed point. Proof: if $f$ had no fixed point, the map $x\mapsto$ (intersection of the ray from $f(x)$ through $x$ with $\partial D^n$) would retract $D^n$ onto $S^{n-1}$. But a retraction would produce a section of $H_{n-1}(S^{n-1})\hookrightarrow H_{n-1}(D^n)=0$, absurd because $H_{n-1}(S^{n-1})=\mathbb{Z}$.
Hairy ball. $S^2$ admits no nowhere-vanishing continuous tangent vector field. The obstruction is the Euler characteristic $\chi(S^2)=2\ne 0$ — a tangent field with a single isolated zero would have total index $\chi=2$, so you can't get rid of the zeros.
Ham sandwich. Given $n$ bounded measurable sets in $\mathbb{R}^n$, there is a single hyperplane that bisects each one. This is a corollary of the Borsuk–Ulam theorem, which in turn follows from the fact that there is no $\mathbb{Z}/2$-equivariant map $S^n\to S^{n-1}$ — a homological statement.

Each time, the argument is: assume the continuous statement fails; derive an algebraic impossibility; contradiction. The main characters in what follows are the fundamental group $\pi_1$ (a refined version of "how loops wind"), the homology groups $H_n$ (counting $n$-dimensional holes), and finally cohomology with its cup-product ring structure. Everything is insensitive to continuous deformation: if $X\simeq Y$ are homotopy-equivalent, all these invariants agree.

2. The fundamental group

Fix a basepoint $x_0\in X$. A loop is a continuous $\gamma\colon[0,1]\to X$ with $\gamma(0)=\gamma(1)=x_0$. Two loops are homotopic (rel basepoint) if one can be continuously deformed into the other through loops at $x_0$. The set of homotopy classes forms a group under concatenation:

$\pi_1(X,x_0) = \{\text{loops at }x_0\}/\text{homotopy}$, multiplication = "first go around $\alpha$, then $\beta$".

The identity is the constant loop; the inverse is the reversed loop. Changing basepoint in a path-connected space gives an isomorphic group (via conjugation by a path), so we usually drop $x_0$.

Theorem. $\pi_1(S^1) \cong \mathbb{Z}$, and the isomorphism is the winding number.

The proof: the exponential $p\colon\mathbb{R}\to S^1$, $t\mapsto e^{2\pi i t}$, is a covering map (see §3). Any loop $\gamma\colon[0,1]\to S^1$ lifts uniquely to a path $\tilde\gamma\colon[0,1]\to\mathbb{R}$ with $\tilde\gamma(0)=0$; its endpoint $\tilde\gamma(1)\in\mathbb{Z}$ is the winding number. Two loops are homotopic iff their lifts have the same endpoint. So the winding number gives a bijection $\pi_1(S^1)\xrightarrow{\sim}\mathbb{Z}$, and concatenation of loops corresponds to addition of integers.

Loops on the circle
drag inside the box to draw · winding number updates live
Draw a loop that starts and ends near the basepoint (the yellow dot). The widget tracks the continuous lift to ℝ and reports the integer endpoint — the winding number.

A few more $\pi_1$'s to remember

space$\pi_1$intuition
$\mathbb{R}^n$ or any contractible space$0$ (trivial)every loop contracts to the basepoint
$S^1$$\mathbb{Z}$winding number
$S^n$ for $n\ge 2$$0$loops in dimension $\ge 2$ have room to slide off
$T^2 = S^1\times S^1$$\mathbb{Z}^2$two independent loops, they commute
$S^1 \vee S^1$ (figure-eight)$F_2$ = free on $a,b$loops no longer commute
$\mathbb{RP}^n$ for $n\ge 2$$\mathbb{Z}/2$a loop is trivial iff you traverse the antipode twice
genus-$g$ surface $\Sigma_g$$\langle a_1,b_1,\dots,a_g,b_g\mid \prod[a_i,b_i]\rangle$one relation from the $4g$-gon

A clean slogan: $\pi_1$ is abelian whenever loops have "room to slide past each other" — true for any H-space, any product, and any space of dimension $\ge 2$ in the sense of CW dimension if $\pi_1$ happens to commute. In general though — figure-eight, surface groups, braid groups — $\pi_1$ is gloriously non-abelian.

Used in
Algebraic topology · Covering spaces
Algebraic topology · Singular homology
Analytic continuation · Monodromy theorem
Lie groups · SO(3), SU(2), and the double cover
Riemann surfaces · Genus as the universal invariant

/* =============================== SECTION 2: winding number widget =============================== */
(function(){
  const svg = $('#wind-svg');
  const W=820, H=360;
  // Left pane: drawing area with unit circle centered at (220, 180), radius 100.
  // Right pane: helical lift of the path to R, shown as a line plot of t -> theta(t)/2pi.
  const cxL=220, cyL=180, R=100;
  const xR0=430, xR1=800, yRmid=180, yRscale=55; // range of the lift plot
  const readout = $('#wind-readout'), count = $('#wind-count');
  const btnClear = $('#wind-clear');

  let pts = [];     // array of [x,y] canvas coords in the left pane
  let drawing = false;
  let winding = 0;  // integer

  function draw(){
    while(svg.firstChild) svg.removeChild(svg.firstChild);
    // left pane: gridded square
    svg.appendChild(SVG('rect',{x:20,y:30,width:400,height:300,fill:'#0b0f16',stroke:'var(--line)',rx:8}));
    // circle
    svg.appendChild(SVG('circle',{cx:cxL,cy:cyL,r:R,fill:'none',stroke:'var(--blue)','stroke-width':2,'stroke-opacity':0.7}));
    // basepoint
    svg.appendChild(SVG('circle',{cx:cxL+R,cy:cyL,r:6,fill:'var(--yellow)',stroke:'#111','stroke-width':1.5}));
    // axes (faint)
    svg.appendChild(SVG('line',{x1:20,y1:cyL,x2:420,y2:cyL,stroke:'#1c2230'}));
    svg.appendChild(SVG('line',{x1:cxL,y1:30,x2:cxL,y2:330,stroke:'#1c2230'}));

    // right pane
    svg.appendChild(SVG('rect',{x:xR0,y:30,width:xR1-xR0,height:300,fill:'#0b0f16',stroke:'var(--line)',rx:8}));
    // horizontal grid lines at integer values of θ/(2π): -3..3
    for(let k=-3;k<=3;k++){
      const y = yRmid - k*yRscale;
      if(y<30 || y>330) continue;
      svg.appendChild(SVG('line',{x1:xR0,y1:y,x2:xR1,y2:y,stroke:k===0?'#2a3242':'#1a1f29','stroke-dasharray':k===0?'':'2 3'}));
      const lab = SVG('text',{x:xR0+6,y:y-3,'font-size':11,fill:'var(--mute)'});
      lab.textContent = `n=${k}`;
      svg.appendChild(lab);
    }
    // titles
    const t1 = SVG('text',{x:30,y:22,'font-size':13,fill:'#fff','font-weight':600}); t1.textContent='path on S¹ (drag to draw)'; svg.appendChild(t1);
    const t2 = SVG('text',{x:xR0+10,y:22,'font-size':13,fill:'#fff','font-weight':600}); t2.textContent='continuous lift θ/2π to ℝ'; svg.appendChild(t2);

    if(pts.length<2){
      const hint = SVG('text',{x:220,y:cyL+170,'text-anchor':'middle','font-size':12,fill:'var(--mute)'});
      hint.textContent='start near the yellow dot, loop around, return';
      svg.appendChild(hint);
      count.textContent = '';
      return;
    }

    // draw path in left
    let d = `M ${pts[0][0].toFixed(1)} ${pts[0][1].toFixed(1)} `;
    for(let i=1;i<pts.length;i++) d += `L ${pts[i][0].toFixed(1)} ${pts[i][1].toFixed(1)} `;
    svg.appendChild(SVG('path',{d,fill:'none',stroke:'var(--pink)','stroke-width':2.3,'stroke-linejoin':'round','stroke-linecap':'round'}));

    // compute running angle θ by summing signed angle increments
    // project point onto circle direction from center
    const thetas = [];
    for(const [x,y] of pts){
      const a = Math.atan2(-(y-cyL), x-cxL);  // in (-π,π], y inverted because SVG y grows down
      thetas.push(a);
    }
    // unwrap
    const unwrapped = [thetas[0]];
    for(let i=1;i<thetas.length;i++){
      let dif = thetas[i]-thetas[i-1];
      while(dif>Math.PI) dif -= 2*Math.PI;
      while(dif<-Math.PI) dif += 2*Math.PI;
      unwrapped.push(unwrapped[i-1]+dif);
    }

    // plot in right pane: x = progression along path, y = unwrapped/2π
    const n = pts.length;
    let d2 = '';
    for(let i=0;i<n;i++){
      const xp = xR0 + (xR1-xR0)*(i/(n-1));
      const yp = yRmid - yRscale*(unwrapped[i]/(2*Math.PI));
      d2 += (i===0?'M ':'L ') + xp.toFixed(1) + ' ' + Math.max(30,Math.min(330,yp)).toFixed(1) + ' ';
    }
    svg.appendChild(SVG('path',{d:d2,fill:'none',stroke:'var(--green)','stroke-width':2.3}));

    // endpoint marker: the integer
    const wind = (unwrapped[n-1] - unwrapped[0])/(2*Math.PI);
    const wRound = Math.round(wind);
    winding = wRound;
    const endY = yRmid - yRscale*(unwrapped[n-1]/(2*Math.PI));
    svg.appendChild(SVG('circle',{cx:xR1-8,cy:Math.max(30,Math.min(330,endY)),r:6,fill:'var(--yellow)',stroke:'#111','stroke-width':1.5}));

    // endpoint-drift readout
    count.textContent = `winding n ≈ ${wind.toFixed(3)} → rounded ${wRound}`;
    const endGap = Math.abs(wind - wRound);
    let msg;
    if(endGap < 0.08){
      msg = `path is (approximately) a loop; homotopy class [γ] = ${wRound}·[generator] ∈ π₁(S¹)=ℤ.`;
    } else {
      msg = `endpoint drift = ${endGap.toFixed(2)} — your path is not yet a closed loop at the basepoint.`;
    }
    readout.textContent = msg;
  }

  function toLocal(ev){
    const rect = svg.getBoundingClientRect();
    const sx = 820/rect.width, sy = 360/rect.height;
    const x = (ev.clientX - rect.left)*sx;
    const y = (ev.clientY - rect.top)*sy;
    return [x,y];
  }
  function inLeft([x,y]){ return x>=20 && x<=420 && y>=30 && y<=330; }

  svg.addEventListener('mousedown',e=>{
    const p = toLocal(e); if(!inLeft(p)) return;
    drawing = true; pts = [p]; draw();
  });
  svg.addEventListener('mousemove',e=>{
    if(!drawing) return;
    const p = toLocal(e); if(!inLeft(p)) return;
    const last = pts[pts.length-1];
    if(Math.hypot(p[0]-last[0],p[1]-last[1])<2) return;
    pts.push(p); draw();
  });
  const stop = ()=>{ drawing=false; };
  svg.addEventListener('mouseup',stop);
  svg.addEventListener('mouseleave',stop);

  btnClear.addEventListener('click',()=>{pts=[]; draw();});

  function presetWind(n){
    pts = [];
    const N = 240;
    // smooth loop: start at (R,0), go around n times, perturb radius slightly to look hand-drawn
    for(let i=0;i<=N;i++){
      const t = i/N;
      const theta = 2*Math.PI*n*t;
      const r = R * (1 + 0.06*Math.sin(8*Math.PI*t));
      pts.push([cxL + r*Math.cos(theta), cyL - r*Math.sin(theta)]);
    }
    draw();
  }
  function presetNull(){
    pts = [];
    // small loop near the basepoint that does not enclose the origin
    const N = 200, x0 = cxL + R, y0 = cyL;
    for(let i=0;i<=N;i++){
      const t = i/N;
      const a = 2*Math.PI*t;
      pts.push([x0 + 40*Math.cos(a) - 40, y0 + 22*Math.sin(a)]);
    }
    draw();
  }
  $('#wind-ex1').addEventListener('click',()=>presetWind(1));
  $('#wind-ex2').addEventListener('click',()=>presetWind(2));
  $('#wind-ex3').addEventListener('click',presetNull);
  $('#wind-ex4').addEventListener('click',()=>presetWind(-1));

  draw();
  // kick with wind-once preset to show off
  presetWind(1);
})();
</script>

3. Covering spaces

A covering map $p\colon \tilde X\to X$ is a continuous surjection such that every $x\in X$ has a neighborhood $U$ whose preimage $p^{-1}(U)$ is a disjoint union of copies of $U$, each mapped homeomorphically by $p$. The fibers $p^{-1}(x)$ are all homeomorphic to a discrete set $F$ — the "sheets" of the cover. Paths lift uniquely (given a starting lift), and homotopies lift too.

Classification (Galois correspondence for covers). Let $X$ be a reasonable space (path-connected, locally path-connected, semilocally simply-connected) with universal cover $\tilde X$. Then connected covers of $X$ (up to isomorphism rel basepoint) correspond bijectively to subgroups $H\le\pi_1(X,x_0)$; the cover is $\tilde X/H$ and its fundamental group is $H$. Normal subgroups correspond to regular covers, whose deck group is $\pi_1(X)/H$.

In particular, the universal cover is the one with $H=\{e\}$. It is simply connected, and $\pi_1(X)$ acts freely on it by deck transformations with quotient $X$:

$\tilde X\longrightarrow X = \tilde X/\pi_1(X)$.

Example: $\mathbb{R}\to S^1$

The universal cover of the circle is the real line, via $p(t)=e^{2\pi i t}$. Each fiber is a coset of $\mathbb{Z}\subset\mathbb{R}$; the deck group $\mathbb{Z}$ acts by translation. This is the cleanest picture of "$\pi_1(S^1)=\mathbb{Z}$": the integers are the deck group of the universal cover.

Universal covers
ℝ → S¹ · tree → figure-eight

For $X=S^1\vee S^1$ the universal cover is the infinite 4-valent tree (the Cayley graph of the free group $F_2$). Every reduced word in $a,b,a^{-1},b^{-1}$ is a distinct vertex, and no nontrivial loop can exist in a tree — exactly what "simply connected" demands.

Connection to Galois theory. The correspondence "subgroups ↔ covers" is a topological analogue of "subgroups of $\operatorname{Gal}(L/K)$ ↔ intermediate fields $K\subseteq M\subseteq L$": fixed subspace = subgroup-cover; normal = Galois/regular; quotient = deck group = $\operatorname{Gal}$. The fundamental group is literally a kind of étale fundamental group, and this isn't a coincidence — Grothendieck unified both under the notion of a Galois category.
Used in
Lie groups · SO(3), SU(2), and the double cover

4. Simplicial and singular homology

The fundamental group sees only one-dimensional information and is painfully hard to compute (it's non-abelian in general). Homology is its abelianized cousin in all dimensions, and the price of abelianization is that we can actually compute it.

Fix a simplicial complex $K$. Let $C_n(K)$ be the free abelian group on the $n$-simplices, and define the boundary

$\partial_n\colon C_n \to C_{n-1}, \quad [v_0,\dots,v_n]\mapsto\sum_{i=0}^n (-1)^i [v_0,\dots,\hat v_i,\dots,v_n]$.

A direct signed-sum check gives the central identity $\partial_{n-1}\circ\partial_n = 0$, so $\operatorname{im}\partial_{n+1}\subseteq\ker\partial_n$. The $n$-th homology is the quotient:

$H_n(K) = \ker\partial_n / \operatorname{im}\partial_{n+1}$.

Elements of $\ker\partial$ are cycles; elements of $\operatorname{im}\partial$ are boundaries. Homology asks: which cycles bound? The Betti number $b_n=\dim_\mathbb{Q} H_n(K;\mathbb{Q})$ counts independent $n$-dimensional holes.

The fact that changing the triangulation doesn't change $H_*$ — and that $H_*$ agrees with singular homology (defined with continuous simplices) — is a nontrivial theorem. In practice we just compute $H_*$ from any convenient simplicial decomposition.

Simplicial complex builder
click a preset · (or click vertices, then edges, then triangles to build your own)

Some sanity-checking values:

  • a point: $H_0=\mathbb{Z}$, all higher $H_n=0$. Betti: $b_0=1$.
  • $S^1$: $H_0=\mathbb{Z}$, $H_1=\mathbb{Z}$. One connected component, one loop.
  • $S^2$: $H_0=\mathbb{Z}$, $H_1=0$, $H_2=\mathbb{Z}$. One connected component, no 1-holes, one 2-dimensional "cavity".
  • $T^2$: $H_0=\mathbb{Z}$, $H_1=\mathbb{Z}^2$, $H_2=\mathbb{Z}$. The $\mathbb{Z}^2$ comes from the two independent loops; note it matches $\pi_1$ abelianized.
  • $\mathbb{RP}^2$ (over $\mathbb{Q}$): $b_0=1$, $b_1=0$, $b_2=0$. The single nontrivial loop has order 2, so rationally it dies. Over $\mathbb{Z}$ there is a torsion summand $H_1=\mathbb{Z}/2$.

  });

    loadPreset(preset.value);
  });

  function loadPreset(key){
    V=[]; E=[]; F=[]; edgePick=null; facePick=[];
    const cx=W/2, cy=H/2;
    if(key==='point'){
      V=[[cx,cy]];
    } else if(key==='segment'){
      V=[[cx-100,cy],[cx+100,cy]];
      E=[[0,1]];
    } else if(key==='triangle-filled'){
      V=[[cx-100,cy+70],[cx+100,cy+70],[cx,cy-80]];
      E=[[0,1],[1,2],[0,2]];
      F=[[0,1,2]];
    } else if(key==='triangle-boundary'){
      V=[[cx-100,cy+70],[cx+100,cy+70],[cx,cy-80]];
      E=[[0,1],[1,2],[0,2]];
    } else if(key==='sphere'){
      // Boundary of a tetrahedron: 4 vertices, 6 edges, 4 faces
      V=[[cx,cy-90],[cx-90,cy+50],[cx+90,cy+50],[cx,cy+10]]; // last is "top" projected
      E=[[0,1],[0,2],[0,3],[1,2],[1,3],[2,3]];
      F=[[0,1,2],[0,1,3],[0,2,3],[1,2,3]];
    } else if(key==='torus'){
      // Standard 3×3 grid triangulation of the torus: 9 vertices, 27 edges, 18 triangles.
      // Each vertex (i,j) for i,j in {0,1,2}; edges identify (3,j) with (0,j) and (i,3) with (i,0).
      V = [];
      for(let j=0;j<3;j++) for(let i=0;i<3;i++){
        V.push([cx - 130 + i*130, cy - 90 + j*90]);
      }
      const idx = (i,j) => ((j%3)*3) + (i%3);
      F = [];
      for(let j=0;j<3;j++) for(let i=0;i<3;i++){
        // each cell gives two triangles with the 'lower-left' orientation
        const a = idx(i,j), b = idx(i+1,j), c = idx(i,j+1), dd = idx(i+1,j+1);
        F.push([a,b,dd].slice().sort((u,v)=>u-v));
        F.push([a,c,dd].slice().sort((u,v)=>u-v));
      }
      E=[];
      const seen=new Set();
      for(const [a,b,c] of F){
        for(const [u,v] of [[a,b],[b,c],[a,c]]){
          const mn=Math.min(u,v), mx=Math.max(u,v);
          const k=`${mn}_${mx}`;
          if(!seen.has(k)){ seen.add(k); E.push([mn,mx]); }

      // faces written with vertices in increasing order:
      F = [
        [0,1,2],[0,1,4],[0,2,3],[0,3,5],[0,4,5],
        [1,2,5],[1,3,4],[1,3,5],[2,3,4],[2,4,5]
      ];
      E=[];
      const seen = new Set();
      for(const [a,b,c] of F){
        for(const [u,v] of [[a,b],[b,c],[a,c]]){
          const k = `${u}_${v}`;
          if(!seen.has(k)){ seen.add(k); E.push([u,v]); }
        }
      }
    } else if(key==='figure8'){
      V=[[cx,cy],[cx-110,cy-60],[cx-60,cy+60],[cx+60,cy+60],[cx+110,cy-60]];
      E=[[0,1],[1,2],[2,0],[0,3],[3,4],[4,0]];
    }
    draw(); compute();
  }

  svg.addEventListener('click',ev=>{
    const rect = svg.getBoundingClientRect();
    const sx = W/rect.width, sy = H/rect.height;
    const x=(ev.clientX-rect.left)*sx, y=(ev.clientY-rect.top)*sy;
    if(mode==='v'){
      // snap to existing vertex if close
      const hit = nearest(x,y);
      if(hit<0){ V.push([x,y]); }
      draw(); compute();
    } else if(mode==='e'){
      const hit = nearest(x,y);
      if(hit<0) return;
      if(edgePick===null){ edgePick = hit; draw(); return; }
      if(edgePick===hit){ edgePick=null; draw(); return; }
      const a = Math.min(edgePick,hit), b = Math.max(edgePick,hit);
      if(!E.some(([u,v])=>u===a&&v===b)) E.push([a,b]);
      edgePick=null; draw(); compute();
    } else if(mode==='f'){
      const hit = nearest(x,y);
      if(hit<0) return;
      if(!facePick.includes(hit)) facePick.push(hit);
      if(facePick.length===3){
        const s = facePick.slice().sort((a,b)=>a-b);
        if(!F.some(f=>f[0]===s[0]&&f[1]===s[1]&&f[2]===s[2])) F.push(s);
        // also auto-add edges
        for(const [u,v] of [[s[0],s[1]],[s[1],s[2]],[s[0],s[2]]]){
          if(!E.some(([a,b])=>a===u&&b===v)) E.push([u,v]);
        }
        facePick=[];
      }
      draw(); compute();
    }
  });

  function nearest(x,y){
    let best=-1, bd=400;
    for(let i=0;i<V.length;i++){
      const d = Math.hypot(V[i][0]-x, V[i][1]-y);
      if(d<bd){bd=d; best=i;}
    }
    return bd<14 ? best : -1;
  }

  function draw(){
    while(svg.firstChild) svg.removeChild(svg.firstChild);
    svg.appendChild(SVG('rect',{x:0,y:0,width:W,height:H,fill:'#0b0f16'}));
    // faces
    for(const [a,b,c] of F){
      const [xa,ya]=V[a], [xb,yb]=V[b], [xc,yc]=V[c];
      svg.appendChild(SVG('polygon',{
        points:`${xa},${ya} ${xb},${yb} ${xc},${yc}`,
        fill:'rgba(88,196,221,0.12)', stroke:'none'
      }));
    }
    // edges
    for(const [i,j] of E){
      const [xi,yi]=V[i], [xj,yj]=V[j];
      svg.appendChild(SVG('line',{x1:xi,y1:yi,x2:xj,y2:yj,stroke:'var(--yellow)','stroke-width':1.6,'stroke-opacity':0.9}));
    }
    // vertices
    for(let i=0;i<V.length;i++){
      const [x,y]=V[i];
      svg.appendChild(SVG('circle',{cx:x,cy:y,r:5,fill:'var(--ink)',stroke:'#111','stroke-width':1.4}));
      const lb = SVG('text',{x:x+8,y:y-8,'font-size':11,fill:'var(--mute)'});
      lb.textContent = `${i}`;
      svg.appendChild(lb);
    }
    // highlight edge pick
    if(edgePick!==null && V[edgePick]){
      const [x,y]=V[edgePick];
      svg.appendChild(SVG('circle',{cx:x,cy:y,r:9,fill:'none',stroke:'var(--pink)','stroke-width':2}));
    }
    // highlight face picks
    for(const idx of facePick){
      const [x,y]=V[idx];
      svg.appendChild(SVG('circle',{cx:x,cy:y,r:10,fill:'none',stroke:'var(--green)','stroke-width':2}));
    }
  }

  function compute(){
    // Build boundary matrices d1: C1 -> C0 (rows=V, cols=E), d2: C2 -> C1 (rows=E, cols=F)
    const nV=V.length, nE=E.length, nF=F.length;
    // edge index map
    const eidx = new Map();
    E.forEach(([a,b],k)=>{ eidx.set(`${a}_${b}`,k); });

    // d1[v][e] = -1 if e=[v,*], +1 if e=[*,v] (we use orientation a<b: ∂[a,b] = [b]-[a])
    // so for edge k = (a,b) with a<b: d1[a][k] = -1, d1[b][k] = +1.
    const d1 = Array.from({length:Math.max(nV,1)},()=>Array(Math.max(nE,1)).fill(0));
    for(let k=0;k<nE;k++){
      const [a,b]=E[k];
      d1[a][k] -= 1; d1[b][k] += 1;
    }

    // d2[e][f] = signed incidence. For oriented face [i,j,k] with i<j<k:
    //   ∂[i,j,k] = [j,k] - [i,k] + [i,j]
    const d2 = Array.from({length:Math.max(nE,1)},()=>Array(Math.max(nF,1)).fill(0));
    for(let fidx=0;fidx<nF;fidx++){
      const [i,j,k]=F[fidx];
      const jk = eidx.get(`${j}_${k}`), ik = eidx.get(`${i}_${k}`), ij = eidx.get(`${i}_${j}`);
      if(jk!==undefined) d2[jk][fidx] += 1;
      if(ik!==undefined) d2[ik][fidx] -= 1;
      if(ij!==undefined) d2[ij][fidx] += 1;
    }

    const r1 = nE>0 ? rankQ(d1) : 0;
    const r2 = nF>0 ? rankQ(d2) : 0;
    const b0 = nV - r1;                     // dim H_0 = dim ker d0 - rank d1 = nV - r1 (d0=0)
    const b1 = (nE - r1) - r2;              // dim H_1 = dim ker d1 - rank d2 = (nE - r1) - r2
    const b2 = nF - r2;                     // dim H_2 = dim ker d2 - 0
    const chi = nV - nE + nF;

    let msg =
      `simplices:  V = ${nV},  E = ${nE},  F = ${nF}\n`+
      `rank ∂₁ = ${r1},  rank ∂₂ = ${r2}\n`+
      `Betti:    b₀ = ${b0}   b₁ = ${b1}   b₂ = ${b2}\n`+
      `χ = V − E + F = ${chi}  (= b₀ − b₁ + b₂ = ${b0-b1+b2})`;

    // sanity: d1 ∘ d2 should be 0
    if(nE>0 && nF>0){
      const prod = matMul(d1,d2);
      let ok=true;
      for(const row of prod) for(const v of row) if(v!==0){ok=false;break;}
      if(!ok) msg += `\n[warning] ∂₁∘∂₂ ≠ 0 — the complex is malformed.`;
    }
    out.textContent = msg;
  }

  // kick off with the S¹-preset
  loadPreset('triangle-boundary');
})();
</script>

5. Mayer–Vietoris

For $X = U\cup V$ with $U, V$ open (or more generally, a suitable cover), there is a long exact sequence of homology groups

$\cdots\to H_n(U\cap V)\xrightarrow{(\iota_U,\iota_V)} H_n(U)\oplus H_n(V)\xrightarrow{j_U-j_V} H_n(X)\xrightarrow{\partial} H_{n-1}(U\cap V)\to\cdots$

This is the topological analogue of inclusion-exclusion: you patch $X$ from $U$ and $V$, and the sequence tracks how the homology of the pieces combines, with a correction term — the boundary map — that encodes what happens at the intersection.

Derivation of $H_*(S^n)$ by induction. Cover $S^n$ with two open hemispheres $D_\pm$, each contractible (so $H_k(D_\pm)=H_k(\mathrm{pt})$), with intersection homotopy-equivalent to the equator $S^{n-1}$. The Mayer–Vietoris sequence collapses in all but two spots, and you read off $$H_k(S^n) = \begin{cases}\mathbb{Z} & k=0, n \\ 0 & \text{else}\end{cases}$$ for $n\ge 1$. The base case $n=1$ is just $H_*(S^1)=\{\mathbb{Z},\mathbb{Z},0,\dots\}$, which you can compute directly.

Mayer–Vietoris for Sⁿ
pick n · watch the LES collapse

The same machinery tells you $H_*(T^2)$ via a split of the torus as two annuli; $H_*$ of any CW-complex via its cell filtration; and generally reduces the topology of a complicated space to its pieces plus a connecting homomorphism.

6. Euler characteristic and a taste of cohomology

The Euler characteristic of a finite CW-complex $X$ is the alternating sum

$\chi(X) = \sum_{n\ge 0} (-1)^n c_n = \sum_{n\ge 0}(-1)^n b_n$,

where $c_n$ is the number of $n$-cells and $b_n=\dim H_n(X;\mathbb{Q})$. The second equality comes from the fact that alternating sums of dimensions in a bounded chain complex are preserved under passage to homology — the same trick that makes $V-E+F$ independent of the polyhedral subdivision.

Classical corollary. For any convex polyhedron homeomorphic to $S^2$, $V - E + F = 2$. This is Euler's formula. For a closed orientable surface of genus $g$, $\chi = 2 - 2g$.

Euler characteristic calculator
pick a polyhedron or surface

A taste of cohomology

Dualizing the chain complex $C_\bullet$ gives the cochain complex $C^\bullet=\operatorname{Hom}(C_\bullet,\mathbb{Z})$ with coboundary $\delta\colon C^n\to C^{n+1}$ transpose to $\partial$. Its cohomology $H^n(X)$ is a refinement of homology with extra structure: a graded-commutative ring under the cup product $\smile\colon H^p\otimes H^q\to H^{p+q}$. For instance, $H^*(T^2;\mathbb{Z})\cong\Lambda[\alpha,\beta]$ — the exterior algebra on two degree-1 generators — while $H^*(\mathbb{CP}^n;\mathbb{Z})\cong\mathbb{Z}[x]/(x^{n+1})$ with $|x|=2$.

Poincaré duality. If $M$ is a closed oriented $n$-manifold, there is a natural isomorphism $$H^k(M;\mathbb{Z})\;\cong\;H_{n-k}(M;\mathbb{Z}).$$ Equivalently, cap product with the fundamental class $[M]\in H_n(M)$ is an isomorphism. The dual picture: a codimension-$k$ submanifold gives a class in $H^k$. For $M=S^n$: $H^0=H^n=\mathbb{Z}$, everything else zero — the duality is tautological. For surfaces: $b_k=b_{2-k}$; for $T^2$ you get $1,2,1$; for $\Sigma_g$ you get $1, 2g, 1$.
Gauss–Bonnet, one-liner. For a closed oriented Riemannian 2-manifold, $$\int_M K\,dA = 2\pi\,\chi(M).$$ The left side is a local analytic invariant (curvature integrated); the right side is a global discrete invariant (Euler characteristic). A round sphere has $K=1/R^2$, $\int K dA = 4\pi = 2\pi\cdot 2$. A flat torus has $K=0$ and $\chi(T^2)=0$, a perfect match.

The story continues from here — characteristic classes, spectral sequences, stable homotopy theory — but the germ is always the same. Attach a functor $X\mapsto A(X)$ that turns continuous deformations into algebra, and the functor will see shadows of the shape you could not otherwise make precise.

Big picture. The leap from $\pi_1$ to $H_*$ was the leap from non-abelian to abelian — from words to equivalence classes of chains. That move made everything computable. The price: information is lost (a knot complement has $\pi_1$ distinguishing knots that $H_*$ cannot tell apart). The trade-off between computable and sensitive is the central tension of the whole field, and it is why $\pi_n$, $H_n$, $H^n$, $K$-theory, bordism, etc. all coexist: each one picks a different balance on that axis.

Algebraic topology as a named discipline goes back to Poincaré's Analysis Situs (1895). Homology was in something like modern form by the 1920s (Noether insisted on groups instead of Betti numbers and torsion coefficients separately). Eilenberg and Steenrod axiomatized it all in 1952. Category-theoretic refinements from Grothendieck, Quillen, Lurie extend the story into derived and higher algebra — the algebraic topology of today.

        if(match===0){ // not opposite

      t.textContent = k==='dodec'?'dodecahedron — 20V 30E 12F':'icosahedron — 12V 30E 20F';
      svg.appendChild(t);
      const c = SVG('circle',{cx,cy,r:80,fill:'none',stroke:'var(--blue)','stroke-dasharray':'4 4'});
      svg.appendChild(c);
      return;
    }
    // rotate slightly
    const A=0.4, B=0.3;
    function proj(v){
      const [x,y,z]=v;
      // rotate around Y by A, then around X by B
      const x1 = x*Math.cos(A)+z*Math.sin(A);
      const z1 = -x*Math.sin(A)+z*Math.cos(A);
      const y1 = y*Math.cos(B)-z1*Math.sin(B);
      return [cx + x1*R, cy - y1*R];
    }
    const P = verts.map(proj);
    for(const [i,j] of edges){
      svg.appendChild(SVG('line',{x1:P[i][0],y1:P[i][1],x2:P[j][0],y2:P[j][1],stroke:'var(--blue)','stroke-width':1.6,'stroke-opacity':0.85}));
    }
    for(const [x,y] of P){
      svg.appendChild(SVG('circle',{cx:x,cy:y,r:3,fill:'var(--ink)'}));
    }
  }

  function drawTorus(k,cx,cy){
    // draw a donut schematic
    svg.appendChild(SVG('ellipse',{cx,cy,rx:110,ry:60,fill:'none',stroke:'var(--blue)','stroke-width':1.8}));
    svg.appendChild(SVG('ellipse',{cx,cy,rx:50,ry:18,fill:'none',stroke:'var(--blue)','stroke-width':1.5}));
    svg.appendChild(SVG('path',{d:`M ${cx-80} ${cy+8} Q ${cx} ${cy+30} ${cx+80} ${cy+8}`,fill:'none',stroke:'var(--blue)','stroke-width':1.3,'stroke-opacity':0.7}));
    const t = SVG('text',{x:cx,y:cy+90,'text-anchor':'middle','font-size':13,fill:'var(--mute)'});
    t.textContent = k==='torus'?'T² : genus 1':'Σ₂ : genus 2 (schematic)';
    if(k==='klein'){
      t.textContent = 'Klein bottle (immersed in ℝ³)';
    }
    svg.appendChild(t);
    if(k==='klein'){
      // draw a bottle self-intersection hint
      svg.appendChild(SVG('circle',{cx,cy,r:14,fill:'none',stroke:'var(--pink)','stroke-dasharray':'3 3'}));
    }
    if(k==='genus2'){
      // add a second hole
      svg.appendChild(SVG('ellipse',{cx:cx+35,cy,rx:14,ry:7,fill:'none',stroke:'var(--blue)'}));
    }
  }
  function drawMobius(cx,cy){
    // a twisted band
    let d = 'M ';
    const R=80, pts=[];
    for(let t=0;t<=1.001;t+=0.02){
      const u=-1 + 2*t;
      const a = 2*Math.PI*u*0.5;
      const wob = Math.sin(a);
      const x = cx + R*Math.cos(2*Math.PI*t);
      const y = cy + R*0.55*Math.sin(2*Math.PI*t) + 10*wob;
      pts.push([x,y]);
    }
    let path='';
    for(let i=0;i<pts.length;i++){ path += (i===0?'M':'L') + ' ' + pts[i][0].toFixed(1) + ' ' + pts[i][1].toFixed(1) + ' '; }
    svg.appendChild(SVG('path',{d:path,fill:'none',stroke:'var(--violet)','stroke-width':2}));
    const t = SVG('text',{x:cx,y:cy+90,'text-anchor':'middle','font-size':13,fill:'var(--mute)'});
    t.textContent='Möbius band (non-orientable, boundary = S¹)';
    svg.appendChild(t);
  }
  function drawRP2(cx,cy){
    // cross-cap schematic: a disk with antipodal boundary identified
    svg.appendChild(SVG('circle',{cx,cy,r:70,fill:'none',stroke:'var(--pink)','stroke-width':2}));
    // antipodal arrows on boundary
    for(let i=0;i<8;i++){
      const a = Math.PI*i/8;
      const x1 = cx + 70*Math.cos(a), y1 = cy + 70*Math.sin(a);
      const x2 = cx - 70*Math.cos(a), y2 = cy - 70*Math.sin(a);
      svg.appendChild(SVG('line',{x1,y1,x2,y2,stroke:'var(--mute)','stroke-width':0.6,'stroke-opacity':0.5,'stroke-dasharray':'2 3'}));
    }
    const t = SVG('text',{x:cx,y:cy+90,'text-anchor':'middle','font-size':13,fill:'var(--mute)'});
    t.textContent='ℝP² = disk / antipodal identification on ∂';
    svg.appendChild(t);
  }

  pick.addEventListener('change',compute);
  compute();
})();
</script>
